glossary.definition: The practice of presenting reasons to support or challenge a position, with the goal of influencing an audience's beliefs or actions. Unlike simple opinion-sharing, argumentation follows structured reasoning and responds to opposing views.

A tradition of argumentation that treats the audience as the primary judge of an argument's quality. An argument succeeds not because it is logically valid in the abstract, but because it resonates with and persuades a specific audience.
The Aristotelian discipline concerned with arguments in which the truth of the premises is self-evident. Validity depends on the correctness of the reasoning, not on the audience.
The Aristotelian discipline concerned with regulated debate aimed at finding a mutually acceptable resolution. Premises must be generally accepted by both parties.
The Aristotelian discipline concerned with the art of persuasion, in which premises need only be plausible enough to convince a specific audience.
A rhetorical strategy that appeals to the audience's emotions, such as fear, empathy, or moral outrage, to make an argument more persuasive.
A rhetorical strategy that appeals to logic and reason, using facts, evidence, and structured reasoning to persuade an audience.
A rhetorical strategy that builds the credibility and trustworthiness of the speaker or writer as a basis for persuasion.

A classical framework for constructing a persuasive argument, comprising five stages: Inventio (selecting relevant content), Dispositio (arranging it effectively), Elocutio (expressing it with appropriate style), Memoria (memorization), and Pronuntiatio (delivery). This dissertation operationalizes the first three computationally.

A form of reasoning that allows conclusions to be revised or withdrawn when new information is introduced. Unlike strict logical proofs, defeasible reasoning reflects the provisional nature of real-world arguments.

A formal model (Dung, 1995) that treats arguments as abstract entities connected by attack relations, without considering their internal content. Provides a unified way to determine which arguments can be accepted given a set of conflicts.
An extension of abstract argumentation in which the strength of an argument depends on the values held by a particular audience, making argument acceptance audience-relative rather than absolute.
A recognized pattern of reasoning, such as reasoning from analogy or from cause to effect, that gives an argument its inferential structure.

A classical rhetorical concept referring to the timeliness and situational appropriateness of an argument. An argument that was highly persuasive when first published may lose force as circumstances change.

A measure of how well an argument achieves its persuasive purpose. In this dissertation, quality is defined not as a fixed property of the argument itself, but as a function of its effect on audiences with different beliefs.
One dimension of argument quality referring to whether an argument is logically well-reasoned: its premises are acceptable, relevant, and sufficient to support its conclusion.
One dimension of argument quality referring to whether an argument contributes constructively to resolving a disagreement, following norms of fair and cooperative dialogue.

The degree to which an argument changes, reinforces, or leaves unaffected the stance of a specific audience. An argument is effective if it shifts the audience's position; ineffective if it produces no change.
An argument is challenging if it causes a reader to reconsider a position they initially held, even if they do not ultimately change it.
An argument is reinforcing if it strengthens a stance the reader already holds, providing new reasons or evidence that support their existing position.
A specific outcome of a strongly reinforcing argument, in which the reader feels better equipped to argue for their own position after engaging with the argument.

The most extreme outcome of a challenging argument: the reader fully reverses their prior position to align with that of the argument. Rare in this dissertation — fewer than 1% of editorial annotations recorded it.

An argument that challenges at least one audience group, thereby reducing the ideological gap between opposing groups. The highest quality is achieved when the argument challenges both groups simultaneously.
An argument that produces no effect on any audience group — neither challenging nor reinforcing — indicating it contains no new or persuasive content for either side.
The distance between two audience groups in terms of their beliefs and positions on a topic. A high-quality argument reduces this gap; a low-quality argument leaves it unchanged or widens it.

The ability of an argument to be persuasive or engaging for audiences whose beliefs differ from those of the arguer. Arguments with cross-ideological reach are classified as high-quality in this dissertation.

Beliefs or principles that a person treats as non-negotiable and immune to trade-off reasoning, regardless of how compelling an argument is. When a topic touches sacred values, persuasion may be impossible regardless of argument quality or rhetorical strategy.

A structured description of an audience's characteristics used to predict or condition how they will respond to an argument. In this dissertation, profiles include political ideology, ideological intensity, and personality traits.

A set of beliefs and values that shape how a person interprets social, economic, and political issues. Operationalized here as a spectrum from liberal to conservative, based on the PEW Research Center political typology.
Two broad ideological orientations in American politics. Used in this dissertation as operationalized reader profiles, not as evaluative judgments.
The degree to which a person holds their political views. Lean profiles hold moderate positions; extreme profiles hold more strongly committed positions.
The challenge of persuading an audience whose beliefs oppose those of the argument's author. Consistently harder to achieve than within-ideology persuasion across all chapters.
A widely used psychological model describing human personality along five dimensions: Openness to Experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism.

A personality cluster defined by low neuroticism and high scores on all other Big Five traits (Gerlach et al., 2018). Readers in this cluster show less responsiveness to stylistic variation than the Other cluster.
A social or media environment in which a person encounters only information and arguments that confirm their existing beliefs, with little exposure to opposing views.

A term from political sociology (Piketty, 2018) referring to the shift in left-leaning political parties from representing low-education workers to representing high-education professionals. Used here to contextualize why NLP models trained on academic and journalistic text may systematically favor liberal-aligned linguistic registers.

The systematic tendency of a language model to generate, evaluate, or classify text in ways that reflect the ideological distribution of its training data. LLMs in this dissertation consistently show a liberal-leaning prior.

The recurring finding across Chapters 3, 4, and 5 that all computational models perform significantly worse for conservative than for liberal audiences. Framed both as a finding about cross-ideological persuasion and as a methodological limitation.
An interdisciplinary field combining natural language processing and argumentation theory to study, analyze, and generate arguments at scale using computational methods.
A branch of artificial intelligence concerned with enabling computers to understand, interpret, and generate human language.
The computational task of automatically identifying and extracting argumentative components from text, such as locating claims and premises and detecting relationships between them.
The internal organization of an argument, specifying which parts are claims, which are supporting or opposing reasons, and how they relate to one another.
A single, self-contained argumentative statement, such as a claim, a supporting reason, or an opposing point. The building blocks used to construct full arguments in Chapter 5.
A short argumentative text consisting of a single argument for a specific topic and stance, typically composed of a small number of argumentative units.
A combination of a debatable topic and a position on it (pro or con). The input unit for argument generation in Chapter 5.

Quantifiable, non-topical properties of a text that capture how something is said rather than what is said. Examples include emotional tone, use of personal pronouns, subjectivity, and argumentative cues.

A psycholinguistic analysis tool that maps words to psychologically meaningful categories such as emotional tone, cognitive processes, and social orientation, producing numerical scores across 15 dimensions.
A LIWC-derived score reflecting how much a text relies on logical, formal language versus personal, narrative language.
A LIWC-derived score reflecting the degree to which a text conveys social status, confidence, and leadership.
A LIWC-derived score reflecting the extent to which a writer expresses themselves genuinely and personally. Higher scores indicate more personal, humble, or vulnerable writing.
A LIWC-derived score reflecting the balance of positive and negative emotion in a text. Scores below 50 indicate predominantly negative emotional language; above 50 indicates positive.

A manually compiled resource associating English words with eight basic emotions (anger, fear, disgust, sadness, surprise, anticipation, trust, joy) and two sentiment polarities (positive, negative). Used to count emotion-bearing words per editorial.
A lexicon of argumentation patterns such as causation, conditionals, contrast, and assessment. Used to count how frequently each argumentation type appears in a text.
A property of a sentence or text reflecting the degree to which it expresses personal opinions or feelings rather than objective facts.
The thematic reach of an editorial's topic, categorized as Global, National, or State. Used to examine whether topic scope conditions the role of style across audience profiles.

The structural segments of an editorial. The lead comprises the opening paragraphs, the body is the main argumentative content, and the ending is the final paragraph. Used to analyze sequential style patterns across the full argument structure.
A characteristic sequence of stylistic properties across the lead, body, and ending of an editorial, associated with a specific persuasive effect.
A tone that swings between emotional extremes rather than settling clearly positive or negative. Identified as a discriminating feature of challenging editorials for liberal readers.
A machine learning approach in which a model learns to map inputs to outputs using labeled training examples.
A supervised learning task in which the model assigns each input to one of a fixed set of categories.
A supervised learning task in which the model predicts a continuous numerical value rather than a discrete category.
The process of manually designing numerical representations of text that capture linguistically meaningful properties for use in machine learning models.
A supervised machine learning algorithm that finds the decision boundary maximizing the margin between classes. Used for persuasive-effect classification in Chapter 4.
A neural network architecture that processes text by learning contextual relationships between all words in a sequence simultaneously. The basis for modern large language models.
A contiguous sequence of n items from a text. Used as content features in text classification and as a unit of evaluation in argument generation.
Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency. A weighting scheme that reflects how important a word is to a particular document relative to a collection.
A model evaluation technique that splits the data into multiple subsets, training and testing on different combinations to produce a more reliable estimate of model performance.
An experimental setup in which one topic is held out as the test set while all others are used for training, repeated for each topic. Ensures no topic-specific knowledge leaks into evaluation.
A model that learns patterns general enough to generate arguments for topics it has never seen during training, by abstracting away topic-specific content.
An unsupervised learning method that groups similar instances together without predefined labels.
A clustering algorithm that partitions instances into k clusters by iteratively assigning each instance to its closest centroid.
A heuristic for selecting the number of clusters in K-means by identifying the point where adding more clusters yields diminishing reductions in within-cluster variance.
A statistical method for discovering recurring themes in a collection of documents.
A widely used topic modeling algorithm that infers the latent thematic structure of a text corpus.
A type of AI system trained on vast amounts of text that can generate, translate, summarize, and reason about language. Examples include GPT-4, Mistral, and Qwen.
A large language model further trained to follow human instructions expressed in natural language.
A method of guiding a large language model by providing task examples or instructions directly within the prompt, without modifying the model's parameters.
Methods for controlling LLM behavior through natural language input. Includes zero-shot (no examples), one-shot (one example), and role-playing (persona adoption).
A method that enhances LLM responses by retrieving relevant external documents and incorporating them into the prompt. Used in Chapter 3 to inject time-relevant ideological context.
A technique that modifies a language model's internal representations during inference to shift its output toward a desired behavior, without retraining.
A vector derived from the difference between internal activations on target-class and source-class examples, injected into the model during inference to shift its output toward the target behavior.
A prompting strategy that instructs a language model to adopt a specific persona, such as a liberal or conservative annotator, to simulate how that persona would respond.
A prompting technique in which a language model is instructed to adopt the perspective of a person with a specific political ideology, validated using the PEW political typology quiz.
An evaluation method in which a large language model assesses the quality of generated text, replacing or supplementing human annotators.
A model that assigns probabilities to sequences of tokens based on their frequency in training data. Used in Chapter 5 to generate structured argument sequences.
A numerical vector representation of a word that captures its meaning based on the words it tends to appear with.
A pre-trained word embedding model representing each word as a fixed-length vector based on co-occurrence statistics across a large text corpus.
A measure of similarity between two vectors, ranging from -1 to +1. Used to measure semantic coherence between ADUs and content preservation between original and rewritten arguments.
The computational task of automatically producing argumentative text, either from scratch or by transforming existing arguments.
A generation task in which an existing argument is transformed to improve a specific property — here, persuasive effectiveness for a target audience — while preserving its original content.
A computational technique that changes the stylistic properties of a text while preserving its core meaning.
Training data in which examples of the source and target style exist separately, without paired instances showing the same content in both styles.
Controlling argument generation based on properties of how the argument is constructed, such as rhetorical strategy.
Controlling argument generation based on the characteristics of the intended reader, such as their political ideology.
An evaluation method in which two outputs are compared directly, with a judge determining which is better. Used in Chapter 5 to compare generated arguments against human-written ones.
The degree to which a generated argument correctly follows its target rhetorical strategy.
The tendency of evaluators to favor a response based on its position in the presentation order rather than its actual quality. Controlled for in Chapter 5 by comparing both orderings.
A structured collection of texts assembled for linguistic or computational research. The primary corpus in this dissertation is Webis-Editorial-Quality-18, comprising 1,000 annotated news editorials.

The annotated corpus created in Chapter 3, comprising 1,000 New York Times editorials each assessed for persuasive effect by three liberal and three conservative American annotators, yielding 6,000 individual annotations.

A publicly available dataset of online debates in which users argue for and against topics, vote on each other's arguments, and optionally declare their political ideology. Used in Chapter 5 to construct ideology-specific datasets.
Annotation that targets properties of an entire text rather than individual words or sentences.
Annotation that targets sub-parts of a text, such as identifying which sentences are claims and which are premises.
The influence of prior information — such as a headline — on how a reader perceives subsequent content. Controlled for by hiding editorial titles in Chapter 3.
The tendency of readers to evaluate content differently based on their perception of the publication it comes from. Controlled for by hiding the source of each editorial.
The gap between when an argument was originally written and when it was read, which can affect how relevant or persuasive it appears.
Distributing annotation tasks to a large number of workers via online platforms.
A small-scale test run of an annotation task conducted before the main annotation effort, to validate that guidelines are clear and the task is feasible.
A method for aggregating multiple annotators' labels by selecting whichever label was chosen most frequently.
A measure of how consistently different human annotators label the same data.
A statistical measure of agreement among annotators that accounts for chance agreement and supports ordinal scales.
Statistical measures of agreement between annotators that correct for chance. Cohen's κ applies to two annotators; Fleiss' κ generalizes to three or more.
An imbalance in the frequency of annotation labels, where one label appears much more often than others.
An evaluation metric that averages the F₁ score across all classes equally, regardless of how frequently each appears. Used throughout to account for imbalanced label distributions.
A simple prediction model that always predicts the most frequently occurring class. Used as a lower bound.
A standardized measure of the practical magnitude of a statistical difference between two groups, independent of sample size.
A statistical test comparing the means of three or more groups to determine whether at least one differs significantly. Used when normality and homogeneity assumptions hold.
A non-parametric alternative to ANOVA used when normality assumptions are violated.
A method for controlling false positives when conducting multiple simultaneous comparisons, by dividing the significance threshold by the number of comparisons.
A sequential step-down procedure for controlling false positives across multiple comparisons. Strictly more powerful than plain Bonferroni. Used in Chapter 3 for McNemar comparisons.
A statistical test for comparing the performance of two classification systems on paired data.
A statistical test used to assess whether a sample is normally distributed.
A statistical test used to assess whether two or more groups have equal variances.
A non-parametric measure of correlation between two ranked variables. Used to quantify the relationship between personality traits and editorial effect annotations.
Conceptual lenses through which people interpret issues based on underlying moral values such as fairness, care, loyalty, or authority. Used as a rhetorical feature in Chapter 3.
A measure of how well a language model predicts a sequence. Lower perplexity indicates a better fit to the training data.
A metric for comparing two texts by counting how many sequences of n consecutive words they share.
A measure of the average prediction error of a regression model.
